prompt.description: The directive for the worker. Follow the worker prompt template from your system prompt: directive sentence, Context (relevant files/branches), Acceptance (concrete done-check), Out of scope (if non-obvious), Report (task-specific items). Workers already know the signal protocol — do not repeat it.

name.description: Friendly slug for the worker (kebab-case). Should describe the outcome, not the action. Good: 'refactor-auth-tokens', 'add-billing-tests'. Bad: 'worker-1', 'fix-stuff', 'task'.

model.description: Claude model: 'opus' (default; ambiguous, multi-file, or debugging work), 'sonnet' (balanced — well-specified routine work), 'haiku' (fastest/cheapest — trivial writes, summaries, simple greps), 'fable' (most powerful — the very hardest problems where opus falls short). When in doubt, omit and let it default to opus.

effort.description: Reasoning effort for the worker. ONLY pass this when the chosen model supports effort — opus, fable, and sonnet do; haiku does NOT (omit it there). 'low' (trivial mechanical edits, summaries), 'medium' (routine well-specified work), 'high' (substantial but straightforward implementation), 'xhigh' (default — complex debugging, design, anything where wrong output is expensive), 'max' (correctness-critical, cost-insensitive). When in doubt, omit and let it default to xhigh.

workspaceOf.description: Spawn this worker INSIDE an existing worker's isolated worktree instead of a fresh one. Use it to review, continue, or fix that worker's work with direct file access — never read another worker's worktree via your own shell. Pass the id of a worker you spawned (attaching to another orchestrator's worker is rejected). Only allowed while that worker is idle (fails with a conflict while it is busy); for plain follow-ups prefer message_worker to the same worker.

collaborate.description: Give this worker the peer tools (list_peers / ask_peer / respond_to_peer) so it can consult — and be consulted by — the other collaborate workers you spawn under you (its siblings). Enable it on BOTH sides of a runtime information dependency: e.g. domain-expert 'providers' plus the 'consumer' that queries them as it works. Leave it OFF for independent parallel work. See the peer-collaboration section of your instructions for when this pays off and how to set it up.

workerType.description: Worker type to spawn (see the Worker types section of your instructions). The type pre-fills the worker's defaults (model, effort, permission mode, persistence) and frames its instructions — fields you pass explicitly still win. Match the task against each type's 'when to use'; omit for a plain general-purpose worker.

text.description: The follow-up directive. Treat this like a fresh worker prompt — be specific. The worker has its prior context but you should still state the new ask clearly.

title.description: Short headline, a few words. E.g. 'Task complete'

body.description: One sentence with the concrete outcome. E.g. 'Auth refactor done across 3 workers — review ready.'

question.description: The complete question, ending with a question mark.

header.description: Short chip label, max ~12 chars. E.g. 'Approach'.

description.description: What this choice means / its trade-off.

options.description: 2-4 distinct choices. The dashboard adds a free-text 'Other' automatically.

questions.description: 1-4 questions, shown to the operator as one banner.

text.description: The report text. First line MUST be `result: ...`, `needs input: ...`, or `failed: ...`. Subsequent lines: what you did, verification, artifacts, out-of-scope notes.

peerId.description: The id of the peer to consult (from list_peers).

question.description: One focused, self-contained question. The peer cannot see your task — give it the context it needs to answer.

answer.description: Your complete answer to the peer request delivered this turn. This is the only thing that reaches the asking peer — plain text in your turn does not.
